claim: The stacking fault energy (SFE) of the steel is 62.64 mJ·m⁻².
evidence_text: with SFE (γ_SFE) of 62.64 mJ⋅m− 2

claim: Deformation twinning is observed after ~28% tensile strain at RT and after ~10% tensile deformation at LNT.
evidence_text: Deformation twinning is found in the specimen after ~28 % tensile strain at RT, whereas it is found in the specimen after ~10 % tensile deformation at LNT

claim: A low-density steel with 0.3% carbon content can achieve a very high cryogenic toughness of about 200 J.
evidence_text: It is reported that a very high toughness about 200 J in the cryogenic temperature could be obtained in the low-density steel with a carbon content of 0.3 %

claim: Austenite (γ) in the solution-treated steel contains 35.92 wt.% Mn, 5.88 wt.% Al, and 0.098 wt.% C; it is leaner in Mo (1.12) and W (0.86) than ferrite.
evidence_text: The compositions of austenite and ferrite were estimated using electron probe microanalysis, with the carbon content calculated using the lever rule [35]. Table 1 ... γ 35.92 5.88 0.098 0.09 0.09 1.12 0.86 Bal.

claim: Ferrite (α) in the solution-treated steel contains 32.44 wt.% Mn and 6.83 wt.% Al, with higher Mo (1.54 wt.%) and W (1.63 wt.%) than austenite, and contains no detectable carbon.
The as-cast ingot was homogenized at 1150°C for 4 h, hot-rolled into rods of 16.5 mm diameter, and then solution-treated at 1050°C for 2 h followed by oil quenching.
evidence_text: The as-cast ingot was homogenized at 1150°C for 4 h, and subsequently hot-rolled (HR) into rods with a diameter of 16.5 mm. The HR steel rods were then solution-treated at 1050°C for 2 h followed by oil quenching.

claim: The density of the low-density steel is 7.18 g·cm⁻³, measured by the Archimedes principle.
evidence_text: The density of the investigated steel, measured using the principle of Archimedes, is reported to be 7.18 g·cm−³.

claim: The solution-treated steel contains an austenite (γ) matrix with approximately 3 vol.% BCC α ferrite; α grains (~4.7 μm) are uniformly distributed along γ grain boundaries, and γ average grain size is ~10.8 μm with abundant annealing twins.
evidence_text: The volume fraction of the α phase is approximately 3%, with an average grain size of about 4.7 μm, uniformly distributed along the austenite grain boundaries. … The average grain size defined with misorientation angle no less than 15° is about 10.8 μm.

claim: The initial geometrically necessary dislocation (GND) density estimated from KAM is 1.6 × 10¹³ m⁻², and the starting material exhibits a very weak crystallographic texture.
evidence_text: The dislocation density in this starting material is revealed by the KAM values in Fig. 1(b), … its average density is measured to be 1.6 × 10¹³ m⁻². … IPF map … indicating a very weak texture in the starting material.

claim: Selected area diffraction along [100]γ reveals extremely weak superlattice spots near (0-11)γ, corresponding to L1₂-type short-range ordering (SRO).
evidence_text: It can be seen from Fig. 1(d) that the extremely weak superlattice spots near the [0-11] diffraction patterns are observed … This weak superlattice diffraction spots have been revealed to be an L1₂ type short-range ordering (SRO) in previous studies.

claim: Satellite reflections adjacent to {200}γ and {220}γ primary diffraction spots originate from lattice modulation caused by concentration fluctuations of Al and C atoms.
evidence_text: Some previous studies indicated that satellite reflections adjacent to the {200}γ and {220}γ primary diffraction spots originate from lattice modulation caused by concentration fluctuations of solute Al and C atoms.

claim: Electrolytic extraction reveals that V is fully dissolved in the steel, while Nb, Mo, and W are partially dissolved, forming MC-type carbide with approximately 0.047 wt.% Nb, 0.017 wt.% Mo, and 0.024 wt.% W.
evidence_text: Based on examination of the precipitated phase by the electrolytic extraction method, it is found that V is completely dissolved in the steel, Nb, Mo and W is partially dissolved …, with approximately 0.047 (wt.%) Nb, 0.017 (wt.%) Mo, and 0.024 (wt.%) W existing in MC carbide.

claim: Dislocation density was estimated from XRD using the Williamson–Hall method with the (111)γ, (200) and (220) diffraction peaks, taking a Burgers vector of 0.26 nm.
evidence_text: The average values of crystallite size D and micro-strain … were estimated according to the Williamson–Hall equation by using the profiles of (111)γ, (200) and (220) diffraction peaks … b is the Burgers vector (0.26 nm).

claim: Tensile specimens had a gauge length of 25 mm and diameter of 5 mm; tests were conducted at an initial strain rate of 1.0×10⁻³ s⁻¹ at RT and LNT, with interrupted strains of 3%, 10%, 20%, 28%, 38% and 55%.
evidence_text: Tensile tests were carried out on standard tensile specimens, with gauge length of 25 mm and diameter of 5 mm … at an initial strain rate of 1.0 × 10⁻³ s⁻¹ at room temperature (RT) and liquid nitrogen temperature (LNT) … interrupted tensile tests to engineering strains of 3 %, 10 %, 20 %, 28 %, 38 % and 55 %.

claim: TEM selected area electron diffraction revealed extremely weak L1₂-type short-range ordering (SRO) superlattice spots and satellite reflections near {200}γ and {220}γ spots, which are attributed to concentration fluctuations of Al and C atoms.
evidence_text: It can be seen from Fig. 1(d) that the extremely weak superlattice spots near the [0-11] diffraction patterns are observed, which cannot be observed in the corresponding dark field image. This weak superlattice diffraction spots have been revealed to be an L1₂ type short-range ordering (SRO) in previous studies [3,14,21,27,38]. Satellite reflections can be observed near some of the main γ diffraction spots.

claim: The Charpy V-notch impact energy was 331±4 J at RT and 254±6 J at LNT, indicating excellent cryogenic and ambient toughness.
evidence_text: The Charpy V-notch (CVN) impact energy reflects the toughness of the material, which is 331±4 J at room temperature and 254±6 J at cryogenic temperature, indicating an excellent toughness at both RT and LNT for the low-carbon low-density steel.

claim: Instrumented impact tests showed crack initiation energy was ~80–100 J for both RT and LNT, whereas crack propagation energy decreased from 251 J at RT to 156 J at LNT, so impact toughness at both temperatures was governed by crack propagation energy.
evidence_text: crack initiation is almost the same of ~80–100 J for both room temperature deformation and cryogenic temperature deformation. However, the crack propagation energy is changed significantly from 251 J at room temperature to 156 J at cryogenic temperature. This means that the Charpy impact at both temperature is governed by crack propagation energy.

claim: EBSD analysis showed a weak initial texture that gradually strengthened to a strong <111>//TA and weak <100>//TA texture with strain at RT; similar evolution occurred at LNT, but the <111>//TA texture strength at maximum uniform elongation was higher due to the larger strain.
evidence_text: It can be seen that a weak texture of the austenite as shown in Fig. 3(a) before the tensile test, but the texture strength increases gradually with increasing of the tensile deformation strain. Finally, a very strong <111>//TA texture and weak <100>//TA texture were observed in the samples tensile deformed at RT as shown in Fig. 3(a). Similar texture evolution is found in the samples tensile deformed at cryogenic temperature as shown in Fig. 3(b). It should be pointed out, the texture strength of <111>//TA texture component at the maximum uniform elongation at cryogenic temperature is significantly higher than that deformed at room temperature, which is clearly resulted from the higher tensile deformation strain at cryogenic temperature.

claim: The average {111}<110>γ Schmid factor at RT peaked near 3% strain while {111}<112>γ decreased, indicating easier dislocation slip activation; at LNT both Schmid factors peaked together at approximately 10% strain, suggesting simultaneous ease of dislocation slip and twinning in early deformation.
evidence_text: It can be seen that the average {111}<110>γ Schmid factor at RT achieved its maximum value near 3 % tensile strain, while the average {111}<112>γ Schmid factor at RT showed a decrease at the same strain level. ... both the average {111}<110>γ Schmid factor and the {111}<112>γ Schmid factor at LNT reach their maximum values at approximately 10 % engineering strain. This means that dislocation slip and twinning are easier to occur in the early stage of deformation at LNT.

claim: At LNT, abundant deformation twins formed in type II and type III grains, with twinning fraction increasing with strain; type II grains tended to form single-orientation twins whereas type III grains formed multiple-orientation twins.
evidence_text: plenty deformation twins could be found in the microstructure when the studied steel is deformed at LNT. And the deformation twinning fraction increased with the increasing of the deformation strain. ... It can be observed in Fig. 5(c2) that type II grains tend to form single orientation twinning, while type III grains tend to form multiple orientation twinning.

claim: EBSD analysis revealed that type II grains predominantly displayed single dislocation slip traces; type III grains showed double glide traces; type I grains rarely showed slip traces. In low-carbon steel, grains near <001>//TA formed dislocation cells instead of Taylor lattice, attributed to low carbon content and high strain rate promoting cross glide.
evidence_text: two types of dislocation glide traces as shown in Fig. 6(a–c) and (d-f), double dislocation glide traces and single dislocation slip traces, are formed. It can be seen that type II grains exhibit a propensity for the single slip traces, Type III grains demonstrate a tendency to develop double glide traces, whereas only a small fraction of type I grains exhibit slip traces. ... in low-carbon low-density steel, the deformation microstructure of grains close to <001>//TA is featured by the formation of the dislocation cells. This microstructure difference ... may be resulted from low-carbon content and high deformation rate, which promote the cross glide in grains with <100>//TA.

claim: In the low-carbon low-density steel, the deformation microstructure of grains close to <001>//TA is characterized by dislocation cells, unlike Fe-30.5Mn-2.1Al-1.2C steel where a Taylor lattice forms, attributed to low-carbon content and high deformation rate promoting cross glide.
evidence_text: the deformation microstructure of grains close to <001>//TA is featured by the formation of the dislocation cells. ... low-carbon content and high deformation rate, which promote the cross glide in grains with <100>//TA.

claim: At RT, type I grains oriented close to <100>//TA are more prone to forming dislocation cells, while type II and III grains oriented away from <100>//TA are more likely to develop cell blocks.
evidence_text: grains (type I) oriented close to <100>//TA are more prone to forming dislocation cells (Fig. 7(f)), while grains (type II and III) oriented away from <100>//TA are more likely to develop cell blocks (Fig. 7(e))

claim: At 28% tensile strain at RT, dislocation cell blocks and cells are refined; a small amount of deformation twinning forms along cell block walls, and a few microbands appear, indicating stress localization.
evidence_text: Fig. 7(g–j) shows that the CBs and DCs are refined as the strain increases to 28 %. ... A small amount of deformation twinning has been formed along the cell block walls (CBWs), as shown in Fig. 7(g and h). Furthermore, a few microbands (Fig. 7(j)) are found, which is an indication of stress localization.

claim: At LNT, the deformation twins formed at low strain are closely related to stacking faults at annealed twin boundaries, unlike RT twins which form via high-density dislocations.
evidence_text: unlike the deformation twins formed at room temperature through high-density dislocations, these deformation twins formed at low strain are closely related to the stacking faults formed at the annealed twin boundaries.

claim: At 20% strain at LNT, dislocation cell blocks form in grains away from <100>//TA and dislocation cells in grains close to <100>//TA, similar to RT; deformation twins coexist with dislocation cells but twin proportion remains low, indicating competition between dislocation gliding and twinning.
evidence_text: Upon straining to 20 %, dislocation cell blocks (Fig. 8(e)), and dislocation cells (Fig. 8(g)), are produced respectively in grains oriented away from <100>//TA and close to <100>//TA, which is similar to observation at room temperature. ... deformation twins and dislocation cells have been developed in the interior of the grains, but the proportion of twins is still very low (Fig. 8(h and i)), indicating a competition between dislocation gliding and deformation twinning in this stage.

claim: At 28% strain at LNT, broad microbands of width ~390 nm originating from grain boundaries and fine microbands of ~137 nm originating from grain interiors are observed, alongside limited deformation twinning.
evidence_text: wide microbands (Fig. 8(l, m)) and fine microbands (Fig. 8(n, o)) are observed. The broad microbands can attain widths of approximately 390 nm, and the fine microbands measure around 137 nm in width, as shown in Fig. 8(l, n), respectively. ... broad microbands and fine microbands originate from grain boundaries and within the grains, respectively

claim: At LNT, a significant number of deformation twins are activated at 38% strain, and a large number of secondary twins are activated at 55% strain near maximum force strain; secondary twins contain more stacking faults than primary twins.
evidence_text: As the strain is further increased to 38 %, a significant number of deformation twins are activated, as shown in Fig. 8(p, q). When the strain is reached 55 %, closing to the maximum force strain, a large number of secondary twins are activated, as displayed in Fig. 8(r, s). Fig. 8(t) shows that, compared to primary deformation twinning, secondary twins contain more stacking faults.

claim: The γ/α interface serves as a crack initiation site during cryogenic impact, causing the impact energy to decrease from 331 J at room temperature to 254 J at liquid nitrogen temperature, a reduction of about 30%.
evidence_text: the red arrows in Fig. 9(g) display the presence of distinct fine cracks around the voids generated by the detachment of α for cryogenic impact sample. This indicates that the γ/α interface is served as the crack initiation site for cryogenic impact sample, which may contribute to a decrease in crack propagation energy at low temperature. The impact energy is reduced about 30 % from 331J at room temperature to 254 J at liquid nitrogen temperature due to the existence of 3.0 % percent of the α-phase in the austenite matrix.

claim: The fracture surfaces at both RT and LNT are mainly composed of micrometer or sub-micrometer dimples, indicating ductile fracture at both temperatures.
evidence_text: Fig. 9(e, j) shows that the fracture surfaces are mainly composed of dimples of micrometer or sub-micrometer at RT and LNT,indicating that both temperatures are ductile fracture.

claim: True stress increases with strain at both temperatures; at 20% strain, true stress is 635 MPa at RT and 1007 MPa at LNT, and reaches 1148 MPa at 28% LNT and 1463 MPa at 55% LNT.
evidence_text: True stress (RT), MPa 339 483 635 723 800 - True stress (LNT), MPa 598 778 1007 1148 1280 1463

claim: Geometrically necessary dislocation density ρ^{GND} at LNT-EBSD reaches 9.73 × 10¹⁴ m⁻² at 55% strain, while XRD-derived total dislocation density at LNT reaches 13.32 × 10¹⁴ m⁻² at 55% strain.

The size of dislocation cells or cell blocks at 20% strain is 448 ± 74 nm at RT and 429 ± 76 nm at LNT; at 28% strain it decreases to 282 ± 56 nm at RT and 242 ± 54 nm at LNT, indicating finer substructure at LNT.
evidence_text: CBs or DCs size (RT), nm - - 448 ± 74 282 ± 56 - - CBs or DCs size (LNT), nm - - 429 ± 76 242 ± 54 - -

claim: The calculated stacking fault energy (γ_SFE) of the austenite is 62.64 mJ·m⁻² at room temperature and 49.46 mJ·m⁻² at liquid nitrogen temperature; the corresponding molar Gibbs energy change ΔG^{γ→ε} is 804.22 J·mol⁻¹ at RT and 576.90 J·mol⁻¹ at LNT.
evidence_text: The SFE (γ_SFE) and ΔG^{γ→ε} of the designed lightweight cryogenic steel at LNT are 49.46 mJ⋅m−2 and 576.90 J·mol−1. It can be seen that the SFE (γ_SFE) and ΔG^{γ→ε} of the designed lightweight cryogenic steel at RT are 62.64 mJ⋅m−2 and 804.22 J·mol−1.

claim: Despite a predicted SFE above the typical twinning range (20–45 mJ·m⁻²), deformation twins are experimentally observed at both RT and LNT, revealing a controversy between SFE prediction and experimental observation.
evidence_text: Austenitic steels with medium stacking fault energy (SFE) of 20–45 mJ⋅m−2 are typically prone to the formation of deformation twins... However, deformation twins are taken place both at RT and LNT, indicating a controversy between SFE prediction twins and experimental observation for our studied lowcarbon low-density steel

claim: Critical twinning stress σ_t is calculated using Eq. (9) with Taylor factor M values of 2.2 (Sachs), 2.7 (CPFE), 3.06 (Taylor), and 3.64 for <111>//TA and <110>//TA orientations, stacking fault energy Γ = 62.64 (RT) or 49.46 (LNT) mJ·m⁻², shear modulus G = 68 GPa, Burgers vector b = 0.147 nm, and grain size D = 10.8 μm; Eq. (10) uses Schmid factor m = 0.326, b = 2.5×10⁻¹⁰ m, and Hall-Petch constant K = 0.350 MPa·m^0.5 derived from yield strength vs. grain size data.
evidence_text: where M is the Taylor factor for a randomly textured polycrystal (M = 2.2 based on the Sachs models [54], M = 2.7 based on the crystal plasticity finite element model [55] and M = 3.06 based on the Taylor model under an assumption of random orientation [54] and M = 3.64 for both orientation of <111>//TA and <110>//TA, T is the SFE (62.64 mJ⋅m− 2 and 49.46 mJ⋅m− 2 at RT and LNT, respectively), G is the polycrystal shear modulus (68 GP [56]), b is the magnitude of the Burgers vector of the partial dislocation (0.147 nm [33]), and D is the grain size (~10.8 μm).

claim: The Hall-Petch constant K = 0.350 MPa·m^0.5 used in Eq. (10) was estimated by linear fitting of yield strengths (267, 258, 251, 245 MPa) against 1/√D for grain sizes of 8.2, 9.3, 10.8, 12.1 μm obtained using solution treatment temperatures of 1000 °C, 1025 °C, 1050 °C, and 1075 °C.
evidence_text: K is Hall-Petch constant (0.350 MPa·m^0.5), K is estimated by linear fitting of yield strength (267, 258, 251 and 245 MPa) and 1/D^0.5 (D is average grain size (8.2, 9.3, 10.8 and 12.1 μm)) using solid solution temperatures of 1000 °C, 1025 °C, 1050 °C and 1075 °C.

claim: The formation of deformation twins in this high stacking fault energy steel is attributed to stress concentration induced by planar dislocation substructures: type II and III grains form cell block walls with planar slip characteristics causing stress concentration for twin nucleation and growth, while type I grains form uniform dislocation cells that disperse stress and are not conducive to twinning.
evidence_text: Type II and III grains form cell block walls with planar slip characteristics, which lead to stress concentration required for twinning nucleation and growth (Fig. 7(g–k)). Type I grains form uniform dislocation cells, which are not conducive to the formation of the high stress required for twinning growth. ... Therefore, the high stacking fault energy (SFE) twinning and its orientation dependence are mainly attributed to the stress concentration induced by planar dislocation substructure.

claim: The presence of short-range order (SRO) in the steel, indicated by diffraction spots but without mechanical antiphase domain boundaries, may reduce stacking fault energy and critical twinning stress, thus facilitating deformation twinning, similar to reports in Fe-22Mn-xAl-0.6C steels and Cu-10Mn alloy.
evidence_text: the microstructure contains SRO (Fig. 1(d)). Furthermore, no typical mechanical antiphase domain boundaries (APBs) bounded by two partials are observed in this steel. The short-range order in our designed steel is similar to that of Fe-22Mn-x (x = 3,6) Al-0.6C steel. Thus, the short-range order in our steel may reduce the stacking fault energy and critical twinning stress rather than increasing them.

claim: Deformation twinning in the present low-carbon low-density Fe-35Mn-6Al-0.1C steel with high stacking fault energy is similar to that found in Cu–10Mn, Fe-22Mn-xAl-0.6C (x=0,3,6), and Fe30Mn11Al1.2C (wt.%) steels.
evidence_text: Such twinning observed in this high SFE steel is similar to that found in Cu–10Mn (at. %) alloy [31], Fe-22Mn-x ( x = 0 , 3 , 6 ) Al – 0 . 6 C [62] and Fe30Mn11Al1.2C(wt.%) steels [27].

claim: The lattice friction stresses are 143 MPa for room temperature deformation and 389 MPa for liquid nitrogen temperature deformation in the Fe-35Mn-6Al-0.1C steel.
evidence_text: The lattice friction stresses are 143 MPa for RT deformation but 389 MPa for LNT deformation as revealed in subsequent calculations.

claim: The sharp decrease of work hardening rate in stage A at both RT and LNT is interpreted by dislocation accumulation and recovery, with the higher rate at LNT attributed to increased lattice friction stress.
evidence_text: This sharp decreasing phenomenon could be easily interpreted by the dislocation accumulation and recovery [30,61]. However, the work hardening rate at LNT is much higher than that at RT, which could be attributed to the increased lattice friction stress with decreasing of deformation temperature.

claim: At LNT, stage B is characterized by a nearly constant strain hardening rate, primarily attributed to the activation of deformation twins at annealed twin boundaries; at 3% engineering strain stacking faults form, and at 10% pronounced deformation twins are evident.
evidence_text: At 3 % engineering strain, distinct stacking faults are produced at the annealed twin boundaries (Fig. 8(b)). Upon reaching 10 % engineering strain, pronounced deformation twins are evident at the annealed twin boundaries (Fig. 8(c)), a result of the previously formed stacking faults (Fig. 8(b)). ... Consequently, the nearly constant high strain hardening rate observed in this stage is primarily attributed to the activation of twin at the annealed twin boundary.

claim: Stage C is characterized by a continuous decrease in strain hardening rate, governed primarily by refinement of dislocation substructure, with type I grains forming dislocation cells and type II/III grains forming dislocation blocks.
evidence_text: Stage C is characterized by a continuous decrease in strain hardening rate associated with the refinement of the dislocation substructure. ... Consequently, the strain hardening behavior in this stage is primarily governed by the refinement of the dislocation substructure.

claim: The α value of 0.4 in the Taylor hardening equation for austenite yields the smallest stress difference (σ_diff) between modelled and experimental flow stress at 10% and 20% engineering strain at RT, compared to α values of 0.136 and 0.26.
evidence_text: when α is 0.4, the σ_diff is the smallest among the three commonly used α values. The contributions of σ_twin, and σ_dis to flow stress calculated with the above method are shown in Table 6.

claim: At room temperature, deformation is primarily controlled by dislocation hardening, whereas at liquid nitrogen temperature a synergistic effect between dislocation hardening and twinning mechanisms is observed (shaded area in Fig. 13(b) represents twin contribution).
evidence_text: At room temperature, only a small number of deformation twins are generated at high strains; hence, deformation is primarily controlled by dislocation hardening, which is the main driving force for strain hardening. In contrast, under liquid nitrogen conditions, a synergistic effect between dislocation hardening and twinning mechanisms is observed (the shaded area in Fig. 13(b) represents the contribution of twins).
